text: The video shows large matted retroperitoneal lymph nodes extending across the central abdomen, located near the aorta, in an elderly patient undergoing resection due to anemia caused by colon cancer.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to perform a lower ligation on the iliac colic pedicle, avoiding the more central superior mesenteric vessels. Large matted nodes are visible beneath the vessel sealer.

The surgeon observes the mid-ascending colon plastered to the side wall, appearing inflamed rather than malignant. Pus is visible, indicating acute inflammation. The retrocecal appendix is identified with a perforated tip, suggesting appendicitis rather than cancer perforation.

The surgeon attempts to mobilize the mid-ascending colon from the sidewall, working on lateral mobilization without ureteral catheters. The hepatic flexure is being mobilized to improve the colon's mobility, avoiding full medial mobilization due to the patient's disease processes.

The surgeon joins the previously mobilized lateral segment, addressing the perforated adhesed area from a medial approach. The right colon mesentery is further dissected, focusing on tumor removal without high ligation, while disregarding the nodal involvement.

The surgeon completes the lateral mobilization, taking additional mesentery to expose clean colon tissue. The perforated appendix is visible hanging down as the surgeon proceeds to address the small bowel mesentery.

The surgeon staples the super pubic region and the small bowel, aligning an isoparastaltic anastomosis. A colotomy and small bowel enterotomy are visible, followed by the closure of these openings with sutures.
The surgeon finishes closing the common channel without leaving a drain, and upsizes the twelve millimeter trocar site for phanaceal extraction.
